SPACEjoint: Integration of Clinical, Spatial, and Molecular Features in Ovarian Cancer

by Venla
22.09.2025

Speaker notes:
This work is part of the SPACE effort to integrate clinical, spatial, and molecular data.

SPACEjoint: Integration of Clinical, Spatial, and Molecular Features in Ovarian Cancer

by Venla
22.09.2025

Speaker notes:
This work is part of the SPACE effort to integrate clinical, spatial, and molecular data.

Project Motivation & Aims

Develop a reproducible R pipeline to integrate clinical data, t-CycIF, and molecular profiles. Specifically focusing on: 
Clinical analysis using features with prior knowledge. 
Perform feature selection and importance analysis to identify informative and non-redundant variables across datasets. 
Construct a patient-level feature matrix incorporating key clinical, spatial, and molecular variables with MOFA (multi-omics factor analysis). 
Identify biological or clinical patient archetypes and their dominant features.

Link spatial/molecular profiles with clinical features.

Project Motivation & Aims

Develop a reproducible R pipeline to integrate clinical data, t-CycIF, and molecular profiles. Specifically focusing on: 
Clinical analysis using features with prior knowledge. 
Perform feature selection and importance analysis to identify informative and non-redundant variables across datasets. 
Construct a patient-level feature matrix incorporating key clinical, spatial, and molecular variables with MOFA (multi-omics factor analysis). 
Identify biological or clinical patient archetypes and their dominant features.

Link spatial/molecular profiles with clinical features.

Project Motivation & Aims

Link spatial/molecular profiles with clinical features.

Speaker notes:
We’re integrating three types of data: clinical info from Oncosys-OVA DB, spatial features from SPACEStat, and molecular profiles from iCAN-DP

MOFA helps us extract latent factors across these datasets

We then use survival analysis—Cox models and Kaplan-Meier curves—to evaluate clinical relevance.

By combining spatial, molecular, and clinical data, we aim to uncover interpretable patterns

Link spatial/molecular profiles with clinical features.

Speaker notes:
We’re integrating three types of data: clinical info from Oncosys-OVA DB, spatial features from SPACEStat, and molecular profiles from iCAN-DP

Feature selection

Visualize the data distribution
Correlation matrices (Pearson, Spearman) for manual correlation check

Speaker notes:
Pearson: measures linear correlation, assumes normally distributed data.

Spearman: measures monotonic (rank-based) correlation, non-parametric, robust to outliers and non-linear trends.

Feature selection

Visualize the data distribution
Correlation matrices (Pearson, Spearman) for manual correlation check

Speaker notes:
Pearson: measures linear correlation, assumes normally distributed data.

Spearman: measures monotonic (rank-based) correlation, non-parametric, robust to outliers and non-linear trends.

Spatial features

RCNs from SCIMAP
Metrics from SPACEstat (e.g. immune-TSI distance, immune infiltration)

Spatial features

RCNs from SCIMAP
Metrics from SPACEstat (e.g. immune-TSI distance, immune infiltration)

Feature selection pipeline

Data cleaning 
Merge different datasets
Combining features and selected them into the actual analysis
Cramer’s V for categorical variables
Functions for VIF, LASSO and Random Forest for automatized feature selection
Features chosen in two or more of these were chosen 
Recursive elimination with repeated cross-validation using C-index (survival outcome) for these selected features

Speaker notes:
Cramer’s V measures the association between two categorical variables (in range 0 to 1) — basically checking how strongly they are related.

If Cramer’s V is very high, close to 1, it means two categorical variables overlap too much. In that case, including both wouldn’t add value, and could actually confuse the model. VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) detects multicollinearity, and I keep only those variables below a threshold (less than 5). That way, features that are too redundant are removed automatically.

LASSO is a regression method (statistical model). It assumes a linear relationship between features and outcome. It works by shrinking coefficients, and keeps only the variables that have a clear linear effect on survival.

Random Forest is a machine learning method and it doesn’t assume linearity. It can capture nonlinear relationships and interactions between features that LASSO might miss.

Feature selection pipeline

Data cleaning 
Merge different datasets
Combining features and selected them into the actual analysis
Cramer’s V for categorical variables
Functions for VIF, LASSO and Random Forest for automatized feature selection
Features chosen in two or more of these were chosen 
Recursive elimination with repeated cross-validation using C-index (survival outcome) for these selected features

Speaker notes:
Cramer’s V measures the association between two categorical variables (in range 0 to 1) — basically checking how strongly they are related.

If Cramer’s V is very high, close to 1, it means two categorical variables overlap too much. In that case, including both wouldn’t add value, and could actually confuse the model. VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) detects multicollinearity, and I keep only those variables below a threshold (less than 5). That way, features that are too redundant are removed automatically.

LASSO is a regression method (statistical model). It assumes a linear relationship between features and outcome. It works by shrinking coefficients, and keeps only the variables that have a clear linear effect on survival.

Random Forest is a machine learning method and it doesn’t assume linearity. It can capture nonlinear relationships and interactions between features that LASSO might miss.

ival.

Random Forest is a machine learning method and it doesn’t assume linearity. It can capture nonlinear relationships and interactions between features that LASSO might miss.

Using different kinds of methods makes the feature selection more reliable, because each method has different strengths.

I then take the features that are selected by at least two of these three methods. This ensures robustness and it’s unlikely that two very different methods pick the same feature by chance.

Finally, I perform recursive feature elimination. That means the model is repeatedly trained to drop the least significant feature, and check model performance with repeated cross-validation. The metric I use is the C-index, which measures how well the model predicts survival.

Using different kinds of methods makes the feature selection more reliable, because each method has different strengths.

I then take the features that are selected by at least two of these three methods. This ensures robustness and it’s unlikely that two very different methods pick the same feature by chance.

Finally, I perform recursive feature elimination. That means the model is repeatedly trained to drop the least significant feature, and check model performance with repeated cross-validation. The metric I use is the C-index, which measures how well the model predicts survival.

Cox model reveals features that predict OS/PFS

The overall survival (OS) probability associated with the RCNs that are related with TSI (tumor-stromal interface)
High RCN10 (TSI-CAF) predicts shorter OS

The PFS & OS probability associated with the RCNs that are related with RCNs enriched in HRP patients.
For subgroup of HRP patients, none of those RCNs predict OS/PFS

Project Motivation & Aims

Link spatial/molecular profiles with clinical features.

Project Motivation & Aims

Link spatial/molecular profiles with clinical features.

MOFA (multi-omics factor analysis) applicability tested

Identifies latent factors that explain variance across different data types

Speaker notes:
MOFA can be viewed as a versatile and statistically rigorous generalization of principal component analysis to multi-omics data. 

Given several data matrices with measurements of multiple -omics data types on the same or on overlapping sets of samples, 
MOFA infers an interpretable low-dimensional representation in terms of a few latent factors. 

These learnt factors represent the driving sources of variation across data modalities, thus facilitating the identification of cellular states or disease subgroups.

MOFA (multi-omics factor analysis) applicability tested

Identifies latent factors that explain variance across different data types

Speaker notes:
MOFA can be viewed as a versatile and statistically rigorous generalization of principal component analysis to multi-omics data. 

Given several data matrices with measurements of multiple -omics data types on the same or on overlapping sets of samples, 
MOFA infers an interpretable low-dimensional representation in terms of a few latent factors. 

These learnt factors represent the driving sources of variation across data modalities, thus facilitating the identification of cellular states or disease subgroups.

MOFA (multi-omics factor analysis) applicability tested

What have been tested (input features, limitations)
Import features: SPACEstat + clinical info. 
Molecular profiles are not available yet (in iCAN-DP)
MOFA works best when 10+ variables per dataset

Speaker notes:
Heatmap shows how the different factors correlate with each other.

Purple intensity shows how strongly each factor is associated with each category.

Each factor represents weighted combinations of features across views

What have been tested (input features, limitations)
Import features: SPACEstat + clinical info.

Speaker notes:

Each factor represents weighted combinations of features across views

Conclusion & future improvements

Achievements: Pipeline built + tested with clinical & spatial features.
Pipeline is available at: https://github.com/farkkilab/survival_analysis. Modularized and can be easily reused. 
Insights: Some RCNs predict survival in subgroups. 
For subgroup of HRD patients, high RCN3 (stroma-residence Immune) & RCN13 (CD4T) predicts shorter PFS.
Still requires more exploration on patient stratification. 
Limitations/Future: Integration feasible but sample size limits interpretation, waiting for molecular profiles. 
Many of these analysis have too many features compared to the sample size. 
Pipeline can now scale once more data arrive.

Speaker notes:
for example for COX-model the guideline is to have at least 10 outcome events per predictor variable
